title: How to Find Community During a Spiritual Awakening
meta_description: Find your people during a spiritual awakening. Real connection without the loneliness. Read more.
og_title: How to Find Community During a Spiritual Awakening
og_description: Find your people during a spiritual awakening. Real connection without the loneliness. Read more.
body:
You're Not Broken. You're Just Outgrowing Everyone You Used to Know.

You know that moment when you're sitting with friends or family, and everyone's laughing about something that used to make you laugh too. But now it doesn't. Now it feels hollow. You're smiling on the outside, but inside you're screaming, "Can't you feel what I feel? Don't you see what I see?"

That's the bright wound. That's the spiritual awakening nobody warned you about. The part where your soul opens up, your sensitivity sharpens to a razor's edge, and suddenly the people who used to feel like home feel like strangers. You're not angry at them. You're not judging them. You just don't fit anymore. And that's terrifying.

I'm Kalesh. I've been where you are. I've sat in rooms full of people and felt completely alone. I've had my nervous system scream at me in crowded spaces. I've asked myself, "Is there something wrong with me? Why can't I just be normal?"

Here's the truth: nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most. You're picking up on things others can't. And the loneliness you're feeling? That's not a punishment. That's a signal. A signal that you're ready to find your real people.

So how do you find community when you're in the middle of a spiritual awakening? How do you find people who get it? Let's get into it. But first, a warning: this isn't going to be one of those fluffy articles that tells you to "just manifest your tribe" and call it a day. This is real. This is messy. And it works.

Why the Old Community Doesn't Fit Anymore

Before you can find new people, you need to understand why the old ones feel so wrong. You might be blaming yourself. You might think you're being too picky or too sensitive. But here's what's actually happening: your spiritual awakening is rewiring your energetic boundaries.

Think of it like this. Before your awakening, you were living in a house with thin walls. You could hear the neighbors, but you didn't really feel them. Now, your walls are gone. You feel everything. The energy of the room. The unspoken tension. The suppressed emotions that everyone else is pretending don't exist. And most people? They're walking around with their walls up high. They're not ready to feel what you feel.

That's not a flaw in you. That's a difference in frequency. You're vibrating at a higher rate now, and lower-frequency environments will feel draining, painful, or even toxic. It's not about good people versus bad people. It's about compatibility. You've outgrown the container.

"Your loneliness during a spiritual awakening isn't a sign that you're unlovable. It's a sign that you're finally ready to be seen for who you really are - not who you pretended to be."

Does that land? I hope so, because it's the foundation of everything I'm about to say. You don't need to fix yourself. You need to find your frequency match.

Stop Looking for a "Spiritual" Community (At First)

Here's something nobody tells you. When you're in the thick of a spiritual awakening, the most "spiritual" spaces can feel the most alienating. Why? Because a lot of those spaces are performative. People using big words. People pretending they have it all figured out. People who are more interested in looking enlightened than actually being present.

You're too raw for that. Your nervous system can smell the inauthenticity from a mile away. So don't force yourself into a yoga studio full of people chanting in Sanskrit if it doesn't feel right. Don't join a meditation circle where everyone's talking about their "higher self" while you're still trying to figure out how to get out of bed in the morning.

Instead, start with the basics. Find people who are doing real things. People who are honest about their struggles. People who don't need to put a spiritual label on everything.

  • Look for local meetups around topics you actually care about. Nature walks. Art classes. Book clubs. Cooking groups.
  • Volunteer somewhere that matters to you. Animal shelters. Community gardens. Homeless outreach. Service work bypasses the small talk and goes straight to the heart.
  • Take a class in something tangible. Pottery. Woodworking. Gardening. Working with your hands grounds your energy and attracts grounded people.

The key here is to stop looking for "spiritual" people and start looking for real people. Real people who are willing to be vulnerable. Real people who can sit in silence without needing to fill it. Real people who don't judge you for crying in public. Those are your people. They might not call themselves spiritual. But they're living it.

How to Read a Room (Without Losing Your Mind)

You're sensitive. You feel everything. That can be exhausting, but it's also your superpower. You can walk into a room and know within 30 seconds whether it's safe for you. Trust that instinct.

But here's the trick: you have to learn the difference between feeling the room and absorbing the room. When you absorb, you take on everyone else's energy. You feel anxious because they're anxious. You feel heavy because they're heavy. That's not helpful. That's just you becoming a sponge for other people's stuff.

When you feel the room, you notice the energy without taking it in. You stand in your own center. You observe. You ask yourself: "Is this environment supportive for me? Do these people feel safe? Do I feel like I can be myself here?"

If the answer is no, leave. You don't owe anyone an explanation. Your nervous system is not up for debate. You're allowed to walk out of a room, a conversation, or a relationship that doesn't feel right. That's not rude. That's self-preservation.

And if the answer is yes? Stay. Lean in. Ask questions. Let yourself be seen. That's how community starts - one small, brave moment of connection at a time.

Online Communities Can Be a Lifeline (But Be Careful)

I know. You've probably heard that "real connection happens in person." And sure, that's ideal. But when you're in the middle of a spiritual awakening and you can't find anyone local who gets it, the internet can be a godsend.

There are forums, groups, and communities full of people going through exactly what you're going through. People who wake up at 3 AM with existential dread. People who feel energy shifts before they happen. People who've been told they're "too much" their whole lives.

You can find them on Reddit, in Facebook groups, on Discord servers, or in niche forums dedicated to spiritual awakening, psychic development, and intuitive sensitivity. The key is to find spaces that are moderated well - spaces where people are kind, where there's no competition for who's "more awakened," and where vulnerability is welcomed.

But here's the warning: online communities can also be echo chambers. People can get stuck in their stories. They can feed each other's fear instead of each other's growth. So use them as a stepping stone, not a destination. Let them remind you that you're not alone. Let them give you the courage to seek out in-person connection.

One resource I've found incredibly grounding during my own awakening is a book called The Awakened Brain by Lisa Miller. It's not fluffy spiritual stuff - it's grounded research on how spirituality actually changes your brain. It helped me understand why I was feeling what I was feeling. If you're looking for something to read that validates your experience without making it weird, check it out: See on Amazon (paid link).

The Hard Truth: You Might Have to Let Go of People

I'm not going to sugarcoat this. Finding new community often means losing old community. Not because anyone's bad, but because you're changing. And change is threatening to people who are comfortable.

Some of your old friends won't understand. They'll think you're "going through a phase." They'll tell you you're "getting too weird." They'll try to pull you back into the old dynamics because that's what they're used to. And you might have to let them go.

That hurts. It's supposed to hurt. Grief is part of the process. You're losing something familiar, even if it wasn't serving you anymore. Honor that grief. Cry if you need to. Write about it. Talk about it. But don't let it stop you.

Because on the other side of that grief is a community that actually sees you. People who don't need you to be small. People who celebrate your growth instead of being threatened by it. People who will sit with you in the dark and not try to fix you.

Those people exist. I promise you. But you have to make space for them. And making space means closing some doors.

How to Actually Meet People Who Get It

Okay, so you're ready to find your people. Here's the practical stuff. This is what worked for me and for hundreds of people I've worked with.

1. Go to the Same Place Over and Over

Community is not built in one visit. It's built in repetition. Find a coffee shop, a park, a yoga studio, a bookshop - somewhere you feel comfortable - and go there regularly. Become a familiar face. Say hello to the same people. Let them get used to you. It takes time, but it works.

2. Be the One Who Starts

I know. You're an introvert. You're sensitive. You don't want to be the one who reaches out. But someone has to. And if you're waiting for someone else to do it, you might be waiting forever. Start a conversation. Compliment someone's shirt. Ask them what they're reading. Say, "I've seen you here before - what's your name?" It's terrifying. Do it anyway.

3. Create Your Own Container

If you can't find the community you need, build it. Start a small group. A weekly meditation circle. A book club for spiritually curious people. A "walk and talk" group where you just walk in nature and talk about life. Post about it on social media. Put up a flyer at your local health food store. You'd be amazed how many people are waiting for someone to invite them.

4. Get Specific About What You're Looking For

Don't just say "I want community." Say "I want people who can talk about death without freaking out. I want people who don't think it's weird that I talk to my plants. I want people who can sit in silence with me." The more specific you are, the easier it is to find them.

5. Let Go of the Outcome

This is the hardest one. You can't force connection. You can put yourself in the right places, be open, be vulnerable, and still not find your people overnight. That's okay. Trust the timing. Trust that the people you're meant to find are also looking for you. Keep showing up. Keep being real. It will happen.

If you want to go deeper into the process of awakening and finding your place in the world, I highly recommend Living Untethered by Michael A. Singer. It's a practical guide to moving beyond the noise in your head and connecting with what's real. It changed how I relate to my own sensitivity. You can find it here: See on Amazon (paid link).

What to Do When You Feel Like Giving Up

You're going to have days when it feels hopeless. Days when you're convinced you'll never find your people. Days when the loneliness is so heavy you can barely breathe.

On those days, don't try to fix it. Don't go searching for community. Just be with yourself. Take a bath. Go for a walk. Cry. Scream into a pillow. Do whatever you need to do to move the energy through your body.

And then remind yourself of this: your spiritual awakening is not a curse. It's a gift. A painful, disorienting, beautiful gift. You are being asked to grow into a version of yourself that can hold more love, more truth, and more connection than you ever thought possible. The loneliness is the growing pain. It's not permanent.

You are not broken. You are not too much. You are not alone. You are just early. Early to the party. Early to the awakening. Early to the truth that most people aren't ready for yet. And that's okay. Your people are coming. They're on their way. Keep the light on.

One more resource before I go. The Highly Sensitive Person by Elaine Aron was the first book that made me feel like I wasn't crazy. It validated everything I'd been told was wrong with me. If you haven't read it, it's a must: See on Amazon (paid link).

You've got this. Keep going. Your community is waiting.

If you're still feeling stuck and want to figure out where you are in your awakening process, I built a free assessment that can help you get clarity. It only takes a few minutes, and it might give you the map you've been looking for. Take the assessment here or check out our quick quiz to see where you're at right now.
